text: To transform CO2 into value-added chemicals and fuels, highly efficient, stable, and affordable catalysts are needed. Here, we show that nanostructured thin films of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) can be used as powerful, atomically defined catalysts for the electrochemical reduction of CO2 to CO in solution. By exploring a range of metal-porphyrin-based linkers embedded in an aluminum-based MOF structure, we found that thin films of Co-functionalized MOF (Al2(OH)2TCPP-Co) showed outstanding performance. By optimizing their preparation via atomic layer deposition-mediated synthesis, we obtained films with a Faradaic efficiency (FE) of 76% for CO2 reduction and long-term stability of over 7 h, corresponding to a site-based turnover number (TON) of 1400. In situ spectroelectrochemical measurements revealed that most of the Co sites within the MOF film are accessible for reduction, indicating that the CO2 reduction reaction occurs at the Co2+/1 redox couple. This study confirms that MOFs could serve as a new family of heterogeneous electrocatalysts, where their molecular diversity can be fully preserved at the material level.
